Navigation:
KEY FACTS — memorize cold:
- Application Navigator (left filter nav) = primary way to reach modules; type to filter by app/module name or URL.
- Banner frame (top bar) = global search, avatar/profile menu, notifications — NOT navigation to modules.
- Favorites (star icon) = personal shortcuts, admin-independent, doesn't change the navigator structure for anyone else.
- History (clock icon) = recently visited records, not a bookmark/favorite.
- List view = many records, columns. Form view = one record, fields. These are two different renderings of the same table.
- Breadcrumbs = show the active filter/condition on a list; click a segment to edit or remove just that condition.
- User preferences (theme, list row count, landing page) = set from profile menu, NOT System Properties (that's instance-wide, not per-user).

COMMON TRAPS:
- Don't confuse 'History' with 'Favorites' — history is automatic/recent, favorites are manual/permanent.
- The banner frame does not include the application navigator itself.
Platform Admin:
KEY FACTS — memorize cold:
- System Properties (sys_properties) = instance-wide config (date format, currency, feature toggles) — affects EVERYONE, not per-user.
- Plugins = packaged features activated per-instance; can add tables, roles, UI, data. Activation is typically one-way (hard to fully deactivate).
- Application Menu (sys_app_application + modules) = what actually builds the left navigator. Admins customize navigation by editing menus/modules directly, not a separate 'nav builder'.
- System Logs (System Log > All) = errors, script output, security events. Filterable by level (Error/Warning/Info) and source.
- Scheduled Jobs = background processes on a timer (e.g. nightly cleanup); admins can view run history + next-run time.
- List personalization (gear icon) = PER-USER, does not affect the list for other users. List Layout (admin-only) = the DEFAULT for everyone.

COMMON TRAPS:
- System Properties = instance-wide. User preferences = per-user. Don't mix these up.
- 'List personalization' (user, no special role) vs 'List Layout' (admin, sets the default) are DIFFERENT settings targeting different scopes.
Users, Groups & Roles:
KEY FACTS — memorize cold:
- sys_user.active = false means the user can't log in, but their historical records (tickets created/assigned) stay intact — NOT deleted.
- Groups (sys_user_group) bundle users for assignment; a ticket's assignment_group is often the real owner until someone picks it up individually.
- A user can belong to MULTIPLE groups simultaneously.
- Roles (sys_user_role) grant capability, checked by ACLs. A user's effective roles = roles assigned directly + roles contained by their groups.
- Role inheritance: a role can CONTAIN other roles — granting one high-level role can implicitly grant several lower-level ones.
- The 'admin' role bypasses nearly all ACLs — this is exactly why it's tightly restricted in real instances.

COMMON TRAPS:
- 'Inactive' user ≠ 'deleted' user. Inactive just blocks login; the record and its history remain.
- Effective roles = direct roles PLUS group-contained roles — don't forget the group contribution when reasoning about what a user can actually do.
Security & ACL:
KEY FACTS — memorize cold:
- ACLs (sys_security_acl) are evaluated at 3 levels: table (all records), record/field-specific override, and individual field.
- Each ACL targets exactly ONE operation: read, write, create, OR delete — a table needs several ACL records to cover everything.
- 'Requires role' = OR logic (any listed role is sufficient). The script/condition = AND logic (must ALSO pass). No roles listed = role check auto-passes for anyone.
- ACLs use table inheritance: a base-table ACL (e.g. on 'task') applies to child tables (Incident, Problem, Change) UNLESS a more specific ACL exists on the child — the more specific one wins.
- The wildcard '*' table ACL = last-resort fallback when nothing table/field-specific matches.
- Data Policies enforce mandatory/read-only server-side regardless of client (browser, mobile, integration) — ACLs control access; Data/UI Policies control state of a field you're already allowed to touch.

COMMON TRAPS:
- ACL vs UI Policy vs Data Policy are THREE different mechanisms with different scopes — ACL = can you see/touch it at all; UI/Data Policy = is it mandatory/read-only right now.
- Most specific ACL wins over inherited/base-table ACL, not the other way around.
Forms:
KEY FACTS — memorize cold:
- Form = sections (grouped fields, sometimes tabbed) + related lists at the bottom (child records from another table).
- Form Design = per-VIEW field visibility (Default, Mobile, etc.) — doesn't touch the underlying dictionary; a field can exist on the table but be hidden from a specific view.
- UI Policies = CLIENT-SIDE only by default — conditionally show/hide/mandatory/read-only based on a condition. Doesn't block a save from script/integration unless paired with a matching Data Policy.
- UI Actions = buttons/links/context-menu items that run a script ON CLICK — can be scoped to form, list, or both; conditioned on role or script.
- Client Scripts = run AUTOMATICALLY on form events (onLoad/onChange/onSubmit) — no click required. The usual place for custom logic too complex for a no-code UI Policy.

COMMON TRAPS:
- UI Policy mandatory/read-only ≠ enforced server-side by default — a script-based save can bypass it.
- UI Action (click-triggered) vs Client Script (event-triggered) — don't mix these up when asked 'which fires automatically.'
Lists & Filters:
KEY FACTS — memorize cold:
- List view columns are controlled independently of the form layout — a field can be a column without being on the form, and vice versa.
- List personalization (gear icon) = per-user, no admin role needed. List Layout = admin-only, sets the default for everyone.
- Filter/condition builder: AND/OR combined with parentheses for complex logic (e.g. State=New OR (State=In Progress AND Priority=1)).
- Related lists can carry their OWN filter, independent of the parent list's filter.
- Breadcrumbs show the active condition in plain language; click a segment to edit, click 'x' to remove just that piece.
- 'Favorite' (one-click save of a filter) is DIFFERENT from saving a full List View.
- Bulk actions (Update Selected, Export) only act on the CURRENTLY filtered/selected rows.
- List Collector = manually-picked multi-record form field. Related list = auto-populated from an existing reference relationship. These are DIFFERENT concepts.

COMMON TRAPS:
- Favorite ≠ List View — a favorite is a saved filter shortcut, a list view is a saved column/layout configuration.
Data Model:
KEY FACTS — memorize cold:
- Every table can EXTEND another table, inheriting its fields AND (by default) its ACLs. Task → Incident/Problem/Change is the classic example.
- Dictionary (sys_dictionary) defines every field: type, length, mandatory flag, default value.
- Reference fields create relationships between tables (e.g. Incident.caller_id → sys_user).
- Dot-walking traverses a reference relationship in a filter/report/script (e.g. caller_id.department.name) WITHOUT writing a manual join.
- Database View = combines fields from multiple related tables into one READ-ONLY virtual table — useful for reporting, does NOT create a new record type (unlike table extension).

COMMON TRAPS:
- Table extension creates a NEW record type that inherits behavior. Database View just presents existing records together — no new records are created.
- Extending a table inherits ACLs by default, unless a more specific ACL is defined on the child table.
CMDB:
KEY FACTS — memorize cold:
- cmdb_ci = base Configuration Item table; extended classes (cmdb_ci_server, cmdb_ci_app_server, etc.) inherit base fields + add class-specific ones — same extension pattern as Task→Incident.
- Relationships (cmdb_rel_ci) capture how CIs depend on/connect to each other (e.g. 'Runs on::Runs') — powers dependency views and impact analysis.
- Discovery / Service Mapping = the tools that populate and keep the CMDB accurate by scanning network/applications (conceptual knowledge — requires real infrastructure to run).
- CI reconciliation rules decide which data source 'wins' when multiple discovery sources report conflicting values for the same attribute.

COMMON TRAPS:
- A CI class inherits base fields the same way Incident inherits from Task — this parallel is exam-relevant.
- 'Relationship' in CMDB context = a specific typed connection between two CIs, not a generic reference field.
Business Logic:
KEY FACTS — memorize cold:
- Business Rule timings: BEFORE (can modify/abort the operation), AFTER (runs once committed), ASYNC (background, non-blocking), DISPLAY (runs on form load, passes values to client).
- Lower ORDER number = runs first, when multiple rules apply to the same table/operation.
- Script Includes hold reusable server-side classes/functions — callable from Business Rules, other Script Includes, UI Actions. NOT directly callable from client-side code unless exposed via GlideAjax.
- GlideRecord = core server-side API: instantiate against a table → add query conditions → query() → loop with next() to read/write fields → update()/insert().
- Events decouple 'something happened' from 'do something about it' — a Business Rule raises an event; separate Script Actions/Notifications react independently.

COMMON TRAPS:
- BEFORE can abort the operation; AFTER cannot (it already happened). DISPLAY is the only one that runs specifically on form LOAD, not on save.
- Script Includes are server-side by default — GlideAjax is the bridge to call them from the client.
Update Sets:
KEY FACTS — memorize cold:
- Update Set captures CONFIGURATION changes (fields, business rules, UI policies) as you make them — NOT data.
- 'Default' update set exists on every instance; best practice = switch to a dedicated custom set before real config work, for easy isolation/review.
- Moving between instances: export/retrieve on target → PREVIEW (checks for conflicts, e.g. a field also changed independently on target) → resolve conflicts → COMMIT (actually applies the changes).
- Preview does NOT apply changes — Commit does.

COMMON TRAPS:
- Update Sets track configuration, never record data — don't confuse this with a data export/import mechanism.
- A conflict MUST be resolved before Commit is even possible.
Service Catalog:
KEY FACTS — memorize cold:
- Catalog items = individual requestable things, organized into Categories. Variables = the input fields on the request form (types: Single Line Text, Reference, Checkbox, Multi-Row Variable Set).
- Catalog UI Policies = catalog-specific equivalent of regular UI Policies — apply ONLY to variables on the request form itself, not to the resulting record's own form later.
- Order Guides bundle multiple catalog items into ONE guided request flow (e.g. new-hire package = laptop + monitor + software access together).
- Catalog Client Scripts = same onLoad/onChange/onSubmit event model as regular client scripts, but scoped to the catalog request form.
- Record Producer = catalog request creates a record on ANOTHER table directly (e.g. an Incident) — skips the standard Requested Item/Catalog Task process. Regular catalog items typically flow into Workflow/Flow Designer for approvals + fulfillment.

COMMON TRAPS:
- Catalog UI Policy vs regular UI Policy target DIFFERENT forms (request form vs the record's own form) — don't assume one covers the other.
- Record Producer skips RITM/Catalog Task entirely — that's its whole point.
Knowledge Management:
KEY FACTS — memorize cold:
- Knowledge Bases (kb_knowledge_base) organize Articles into Categories for browsing AND permission scoping.
- Article visibility restricted by BOTH the knowledge base's permissions AND the article's own audience setting.
- Article states: Draft → Published → Retired. Only PUBLISHED articles are generally visible/searchable to end users.
- Users can rate/comment on articles (feedback) — helps content owners find articles needing updates.
- Search relevance = keyword matching + usage signals (view count, rating).
- Articles can link directly to Incidents/Cases so agents attach relevant content while resolving a ticket.

COMMON TRAPS:
- Draft articles are NOT visible to normal end users — only Published are, by default.
- Visibility is a combination of TWO permission layers (KB-level + article-level), not just one.
Notifications:
KEY FACTS — memorize cold:
- Notifications (sysevent_email_action) triggered by either a record event (e.g. 'Incident updated') OR a named system Event (raised via gs.eventQueue() or similar).
- Email Notifications reference an Email Template for layout/content — one template reusable across multiple notification triggers.
- 'When to send' conditions (fields changed, field values) control whether a notification actually fires for a given update — not every trigger match sends the notification.

COMMON TRAPS:
- Record-triggered vs Event-triggered notifications are TWO different trigger mechanisms — know the distinction.
- A notification record existing doesn't guarantee it fires — the 'When to send' condition gates it.
Reporting:
KEY FACTS — memorize cold:
- Reports pull from ONE table (or a database view). Types: List, Bar, Pie, Line/Trend — pick based on what you're showing (trend needs time-based grouping; breakdown-by-category suits bar/pie).
- Report Designer = no-code: pick data source, group/aggregate fields, filter conditions.
- Dashboards combine multiple reports + widgets (Single Score, List widget) onto one page.
- Report/dashboard SHARING is a SEPARATE permission layer from the ACLs on the underlying data.
- Reports can be SCHEDULED for recurring email delivery — no login required to receive them.

COMMON TRAPS:
- Sharing a report doesn't bypass the underlying data's ACLs for the viewer — two separate permission layers.
- A report can only pull from ONE table directly; a database view is the workaround for cross-table reporting.
Data Import:
KEY FACTS — memorize cold:
- Import Sets land external data in a STAGING table (Import Set Table) before it ever touches real data tables.
- Data Sources define where the data comes from (file upload, JDBC, LDAP, etc.) and how it loads into staging.
- Transform Maps define staging→target field mapping, including Transform Scripts (custom logic during transform, e.g. reference lookup by name instead of sys_id).
- Coalesce field = 'if a record with this value already exists, UPDATE instead of duplicating' — essential for repeated imports of the same source.
- Scheduled Imports automate the whole pipeline (fetch → stage → transform) on a recurring timer.

COMMON TRAPS:
- Data NEVER goes straight from source to target table — staging is always the intermediate step.
- Without a coalesce field, a repeated import creates DUPLICATES instead of updates.
Flow Designer:
KEY FACTS — memorize cold:
- Flow = top-level automation, built from Actions (single steps) and optionally Subflows (a flow called by another flow, for reuse).
- Every flow starts with a TRIGGER (record created/updated on a table, or a catalog item ordered) that decides WHEN it runs.
- Standard logic blocks: If/Else branches, For Each loops — same role as a Business Rule's script logic, but visual instead of JavaScript.
- Flow Designer is the MODERN replacement for the legacy Workflow Editor (still present on some old instances, not used for new work).
- A flow must be explicitly ACTIVATED before its trigger fires live — same idea as an Update Set needing to be Committed.

COMMON TRAPS:
- Flow vs Action vs Subflow are three DIFFERENT levels — a Subflow is reusable and called BY a flow, not a flow itself.
- An unactivated flow's trigger never fires, even if fully built.
Scripting & Automation:
KEY FACTS — memorize cold:
- Client Scripts run in the BROWSER on form events: onLoad, onChange, onSubmit.
- Script Includes hold reusable SERVER-SIDE logic; callable from client-side ONLY via GlideAjax (never directly).
- GlideRecord = server-side query/insert/update API: query() then next() to iterate.
- The client/server scripting boundary is a recurring exam theme: know which API runs where.

COMMON TRAPS:
- g_form is a CLIENT-side API (browser); GlideRecord is SERVER-side. Mixing these up is the most common scripting-topic mistake.
- A Script Include with no GlideAjax bridge is invisible to client scripts.
Collaboration:
KEY FACTS — memorize cold:
- Connect Chat = real-time messaging tied to a SPECIFIC record — the conversation stays attached to that record's context.
- Live Feed = a more public, timeline-style activity stream (not record-specific in the same tightly-scoped way as Connect).
- @mentions notify a specific user within either tool.

COMMON TRAPS:
- Connect Chat is record-scoped; Live Feed is broader/public — don't treat them as interchangeable.
Virtual Agent:
KEY FACTS — memorize cold:
- Virtual Agent uses TOPICS (conversation flows) built in the Virtual Agent Designer.
- Topics are triggered by NLU intents OR direct topic selection by the user.
- A topic must be PUBLISHED/activated before it responds in the live chat widget.

COMMON TRAPS:
- 'Topic' in Virtual Agent context means a conversation flow — NOT the same as a Topics-feature study topic in this app, and not the same as a ServiceNow 'topic' in any other product area. Context matters on the real exam.
ITSM & SLA:
KEY FACTS — memorize cold:
- Incident / Problem / Change all extend the base Task table (see Data Model topic) and share core fields.
- SLA Definitions specify START / PAUSE / STOP conditions and a target duration.
- An SLA's running state (breach %, warning threshold) is visible via the SLA related list on the record.
- Changing certain field values (e.g. State = On Hold, if configured) can PAUSE an SLA timer — governed entirely by the SLA Definition's own conditions.

COMMON TRAPS:
- The record's SLA related list shows the RUNNING INSTANCE; the actual rules live on the separate SLA Definition record — know both exist.
Testing/ATF:
KEY FACTS — memorize cold:
- Automated Test Framework (ATF) tests are built from TEST STEPS: form actions (open, set field, submit) chained with ASSERTION steps (verify a value/state).
- Test Suites group multiple individual Tests for batch running.
- A test without an assertion step only performs actions — it doesn't actually verify anything.

COMMON TRAPS:
- Test (single scenario) vs Test Suite (a batch of tests) — know which term applies when a question describes running multiple tests together.
Agile/VTB:
KEY FACTS — memorize cold:
- Agile Development uses Stories, Epics, and Sprints for planning.
- Visual Task Board (VTB) = a Kanban-style board; columns map to real field VALUES on the underlying table (e.g. State) — dragging a card between columns updates that field.
- VTB is a VIEW/interaction layer, not a separate planning structure — it reflects records that already exist.

COMMON TRAPS:
- VTB board columns vs Sprint/Story planning are DIFFERENT views of related but distinct data — a VTB board is not itself a Sprint.
